      "それは王朝の起源と支配の重要性を視聴者に思い出させるために作成されました。",

About: It is about the Timurid dynasty.
AssociatedWith: It is associated with the reign of Humayun.
It is associated with the reign of Jahangir.
It is associated with the reign of Shah Jahan.
It is associated with the reign of Akbar.
It is associated with Timurid–Mughal dynastic continuity and legitimacy.
It is associated with early Mughal political imagery.
It is associated with Mughal dynastic portraiture.
It is associated with the Timurid illustrative models.
It is associated with the emerging imperial Mughal aesthetic.
ConsistsOf: It consists of figures including Kamran Mirza, Babur Shah, Shaykh Omar, Sultan Abu Sa‘id, Sultan Muhammad, Miran Shah, Abu Bakr Mirza, Baisunghur Mirza, Khusrau Mirza, Mirza Shah Rukh, Sultan Parviz, Shah Jahan, and Humayun.
It consists of attendants labeled "Hiranand," "Inayat," and "Balchand."
It consists of artists including "Bichitr," "Govardhana," "Dawlat," "Mohan," and "Nanha."
CreatedAround: It was created around the reign of Humayun.
CreatedTo: It was created to assert Timurid–Mughal dynastic continuity and legitimacy.
It was created to portray dynastic hierarchy and legitimacy.
It was created to show political continuity and status.
It was created to remind viewers of the dynasty's origins and the importance of its rule.
It was created to display members of the Timurid dynasty together.
HasA: It has a formal garden setting.
It has a pavilion in the center.
It has figures arranged intricately around Humayun.
It has Persian inscriptions identifying each figure.
It has abrasions on the surface.
It has small areas of pigment loss.
It has been trimmed on all four sides.
It has associated losses to the original border decoration and parts of the composition.
HasFeatureOf: It is characterized by opaque watercolor (gouache) on a cotton cloth.
It is characterized by dimensions of 108.00 cm in width and 108.05 cm in height.
It is characterized by damage, with parts cut away and lost.
It is characterized by the central placement of Humayun.
It is characterized by the seating arrangement of Akbar, Jahangir, and Shah Jahan opposite Humayun.
It is characterized by the seating arrangement of Abu Bakr Mirza, Baysunghur, and Shahrukh Mirza to the right of the pavilion.
It is characterized by its interpretation as a symbol of paradise, political order, and dynastic unity.
It is characterized by its importance as a document of early Mughal political imagery.
It is characterized by its adaptation of Timurid illustrative models with the emerging imperial Mughal aesthetic.
It is characterized by its influence on subsequent developments in imperial pictorial production.
HeldIn: It was held in the British Museum.
Influenced: It influenced subsequent developments in imperial pictorial production.
InfluencedBy: It was influenced by Timurid illustrative models.
IsA: It is a group portrait of the Timurid dynasty.
It is an important document of early Mughal political imagery.
It is an early example of Mughal dynastic portraiture.
It is an early example of Mughal political imagery.
It is a piece of work.
KnownFor: It is known for asserting Timurid–Mughal dynastic continuity and legitimacy.
It is known for portraying dynastic hierarchy and legitimacy.
It is known for its visual communication of political continuity and status.
It is known for its symbolism of paradise, political order, and dynastic unity.
It is known for its adaptation of Timurid illustrative models with the emerging imperial Mughal aesthetic.
It is known for its foundational importance in early Mughal dynastic portraiture and political imagery.
LocatedIn: It is located in the British Museum.
OccurredIn: It occurred in the reign of Humayun.
It occurred in the reign of Jahangir.
It occurred in the reign of Shah Jahan.
It occurred in the reign of Akbar.
UsedAs: It is used as a reminder of the dynasty's origins and the importance of its rule.
It is used as a symbol of paradise, political order, and dynastic unity.
UsedFor: It is used for asserting Timurid–Mughal dynastic continuity and legitimacy.
It is used for portraying dynastic hierarchy and legitimacy.
It is used for showing political continuity and status.
It is used for the same political message.
UsedIn: It is used in the British Museum.
It is used in restoration campaigns.
UsedTo: It is used to show political continuity and status.
It is used to remind viewers of the dynasty's origins and why its rule mattered.
